expanded_description: Converting between moles and mass using molar mass as a conversion factor is fundamental to quantitative chemistry and stoichiometric calculations.

core_concept.principle: Molar mass serves as the conversion factor between amount in moles and mass in grams, enabling interconversion using the relationship $m = n \times M$.
core_concept.explanation: Since molar mass represents the mass of one mole of a substance (in g/mol), multiplying moles by molar mass gives mass, while dividing mass by molar mass gives moles. This conversion is essential for laboratory measurements and reaction calculations.

image.alt: Diagram showing conversion from moles to mass using molar mass
image.caption: Molar mass converts between the amount of substance (moles) and measurable mass (grams)

sections.introduction: In the laboratory, we measure substances by mass using balances, but chemical reactions occur in mole ratios. Molar mass bridges this gap, allowing us to convert between what we measure (grams) and what reactions require (moles).

key_concept.definition: The fundamental relationship: $m = n \times M$, where $m$ is mass in grams, $n$ is amount in moles, and $M$ is molar mass in g/mol.
key_concept.context: This equation can be rearranged to solve for any variable: $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ or $M = \dfrac{m}{n}$.

principles.statement: Molar mass acts as a conversion factor with units g/mol, allowing dimensional analysis between moles and grams.
principles.application: Set up conversions so that unwanted units cancel, leaving the desired unit.
principles.example: To convert $2.5$ mol NaCl to grams: $2.5 \text{ mol} \times \dfrac{58.44 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 146.1 \text{ g}$
principles.analogy: Like using an exchange rate to convert between currencies, molar mass converts between moles and grams.

components.definition: The mole-mass conversion requires three quantities: mass ($m$), moles ($n$), and molar mass ($M$).
components.interpretation: Given any two quantities, the third can be calculated. For mole-to-mass conversions, multiply moles by molar mass.

image.alt: Triangle diagram showing m, n, and M relationships
image.caption: Cover the unknown variable to see the calculation needed

worked_examples.title: Converting Moles to Mass for an Element
worked_examples.problem: What is the mass in grams of $3.25$ moles of aluminum (Al)?

step_by_step.explanation: Given: $n = 3.25 \text{ mol}$; need molar mass of Al from periodic table
Use $m = n \times M$ to convert moles to mass
Multiply moles by molar mass; units of mol cancel, leaving grams
The given value has 3 significant figures, so round the answer accordingly

worked_examples.concept_applied: Mole-to-mass conversion uses $m = n \times M$ with molar mass from the periodic table.

sections.section_title: Converting Moles to Mass for Compounds
sections.key_concept: For compounds, first calculate the molar mass by summing atomic masses, then use the same conversion formula as for elements.

classifications.value: Use molecular formula to calculate molar mass before conversion
classifications.behavior: Calculate molar mass by summing all atoms in the molecular formula

classifications.value: Use formula unit to calculate molar mass before conversion
classifications.behavior: Calculate molar mass by summing all ions in the formula unit

categories.description: Hydrated compounds contain water molecules in their crystal structure, which must be included in molar mass calculations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The water molecules (indicated by ·$n\text{H}_2\text{O}$) contribute to the total molar mass and must be counted when converting moles to mass.

examples.explanation: Include all 7 water molecules in molar mass: $M = 246.52 \text{ g/mol}$

worked_examples.title: Converting Moles to Mass for a Compound
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass in grams of $0.750$ moles of sodium sulfate ($\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$).

step_by_step.explanation: Sum atomic masses: $2$ Na, $1$ S, $4$ O

step_by_step.step: Set up the conversion using $m = n \times M$
step_by_step.explanation: Multiply moles by molar mass to get mass in grams
Multiply and round to 3 significant figures (matching the given moles)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Compound molar mass must be calculated first, then used in $m = n \times M$.

worked_examples.title: Converting Moles to Mass for a Hydrate
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is needed to obtain $0.500$ moles of the hydrate?

step_by_step.step: Calculate molar mass including water molecules
step_by_step.explanation: $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$: Cu + S + 4O + 5($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
Use $m = n \times M$ with the hydrate molar mass
Multiply and round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydrate molar mass includes water molecules; conversion formula remains $m = n \times M$.

sections.introduction: Dimensional analysis (factor-label method) ensures correct unit cancellation and helps prevent calculation errors in mole-mass conversions.

key_concept.definition: Set up conversion factors so that unwanted units cancel: $\text{moles} \times \dfrac{\text{grams}}{\text{mole}} = \text{grams}$
key_concept.context: Always write units in calculations to verify that the setup is correct before computing numerical values.

principles.statement: Arrange conversion factors so that the unit you want to eliminate appears in both numerator and denominator.
principles.application: For mole-to-mass: place moles in denominator of molar mass to cancel with given moles.
principles.analogy: Like canceling common factors in fractions, units cancel when they appear in both numerator and denominator.

worked_examples.title: Using Dimensional Analysis for Mole-to-Mass
worked_examples.problem: Convert $1.85$ moles of magnesium nitrate, $\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2$, to grams using dimensional analysis.

step_by_step.explanation: Mg + 2N + 6O (distribute subscript 2 to both N and O in nitrate)

step_by_step.step: Set up dimensional analysis with units
step_by_step.explanation: Arrange so moles cancel, leaving grams
Moles cancel; multiply numerical values
Round to 3 significant figures (from 1.85 mol)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Dimensional analysis ensures correct unit setup: $\text{mol} \times \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mol}} = \text{g}$

worked_examples.title: Comparing Masses of Equal Mole Amounts
worked_examples.problem: Which has greater mass: $2.0$ moles of $\text{O}_2$ or $2.0$ moles of $\text{N}_2$?

step_by_step.explanation: Oxygen gas is diatomic: $2 \times 16.00 = 32.00 \text{ g/mol}$
Nitrogen gas is diatomic: $2 \times 14.01 = 28.02 \text{ g/mol}$

step_by_step.step: Convert both to mass and compare
step_by_step.explanation: Same moles, different molar masses → different masses

worked_examples.final_answer: $2.0$ moles of $\text{O}_2$ ($64$ g) has greater mass than $2.0$ moles of $\text{N}_2$ ($56$ g).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Equal mole amounts have different masses when molar masses differ.

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.key_concept: Understanding common mistakes in mole-mass conversions helps ensure accurate calculations in laboratory and exam settings.

classifications.value: Using atomic mass instead of molecular/compound molar mass
classifications.behavior: Results in systematically wrong answers, often off by factors of 2 or more

examples.explanation: Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
Must count 2 H atoms: $2 \times 1.008 + 16.00 = 18.02$ g/mol, not $1.008 + 16.00$

classifications.value: Multiplying by mol/g instead of g/mol
classifications.behavior: Produces answers with incorrect units (mol²/g instead of g)

examples.explanation: $2.5 \text{ mol} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{58.44 \text{ g}}$ gives mol²/g (nonsense units)
$2.5 \text{ mol} \times \dfrac{58.44 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$ gives g (correct units)

classifications.value: Reporting too many or too few significant figures
classifications.behavior: Reduces accuracy and may cause point deductions in assessments

examples.explanation: Reporting $87.685$ g when given $3.25$ mol (3 sig figs) → should be $87.7$ g
Reporting $88$ g loses information; $87.7$ g properly reflects measurement precision

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting a Conversion Error
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated the mass of $1.5$ moles of $\text{CO}_2$ as $0.034$ g. Identify the error and calculate the correct mass.

step_by_step.explanation: The answer is far too small; student likely divided instead of multiplied, or inverted molar mass
Multiply moles by molar mass (not divide)
Round to 2 significant figures (from 1.5 mol)

worked_examples.final_answer: Correct mass is $66$ g of $\text{CO}_2$. Error: student divided instead of multiplying.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always verify that units cancel correctly and that the answer magnitude is reasonable.

summary: Mole-to-mass conversions use the relationship $m = n \times M$, where molar mass ($M$) serves as the conversion factor between amount in moles ($n$) and mass in grams ($m$). For elements, molar mass comes directly from the periodic table. For compounds, calculate molar mass by summing atomic masses according to the chemical formula, including water molecules in hydrates. Always use dimensional analysis to verify unit cancellation: $\text{mol} \times \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mol}} = \text{g}$. Common errors include using incorrect molar masses, inverting conversion factors, and mishandling significant figures. Mastery of mole-to-mass conversions is essential for stoichiometry, solution preparation, and all quantitative chemical work.
